What happens typically with standards, right? You know, standards, it's like, it's a malleable thing. You know, your standards are not a constant throughout your whole life. We have times where our standards are very high and we have sometimes where our standards are on the lower side. They just like, you know, we are peaking where like in that flow we're doing great, our standards are high and then we have other times throughout like, you know, even over years meaning where you're just not burning as bright in your own life. You don't, it feels as directed. You don't really feel like you're firing at all cylinders. And what happens is this, and here's the interesting part, is that your standards, believe it or not, are more of a, they're almost a result. Your standards are a result of other things that have happened to you in your life. And then what happens is your brain to avoid feeling pain and to maximize feeling pleasure. And just, I've never ever had this, this is a thought, this thought like had just occurred to me this second as I was saying it. Like, because I was trying to think of a way, I had the thought I was trying to get out to. So this is really something I never really said before to anybody, but it just occurred to me in this moment what's really going on. I was just trying to get to the bottom of Fernando's issue. So what, not that it's a new idea, but it's a new way of explaining the idea, right? Because I didn't invent standards, right? No one has, it's been around forever. So here's what's really happening. I think this is going to help you Fernando and everybody else to really start, it's almost a starting point of how to raise your standards. What happens is, is that your standard, right, is like your thermostat. It's your set point of like what you will accept in your life. Like, well, I will not settle for less than X. So like say my standard for making money, my standard is I want to make $10 million a year. That's a very reasonable, rational standard for me, meaning I want to have that much money. And that doesn't mean I have always made that much money at times I've made a lot more and times I've made less. The point is when I'm making less than $10 million a year, I'm not living the life day to day that I feel I deserve and want and that makes me feel great inside. In other words, it's just like, there's this things I can't have access to. I'd like to be able to take private checks everywhere. I'd like to do this or do that, you know, whatever it might be, contribute to charities in a certain way, whatever it might be, like, well, I must. Like, if I get the $10 million in my head, both logically when I think it through and emotionally just how I feel when I wake up and when I'm like, that's a good salary. There's nothing I can't do with that amount of money. That's my standard for me, right? But what gave me that stand? Why is that? Like what caused that to be $10 million, right? And what would cause it to drop down to, let's say, a million bucks and what would cause it to go up to $100 million? What would cause those things to change? And here's what you need to realize here. This is important. This is the crux of it all is that your standard is a function of what your brain thinks is possible and then affected by what's actually happened in the past, meaning those, let's say you think, I think I'm worthy of $10 million. Like I have talent. I have special skills. I have a history of making money. I know the rules of success in my own mind, logically speaking. I can't think of one reason why I shouldn't make $10 million a year. Combine with the fact that I've had times that made a lot more than $10 million and then at times when I've had problems, when I was making almost zero. But here's what happened. I never let the pain of dropping below my standard. So let's say in those years when I was in jail or when I was just having bad years in business, I just couldn't find my sweet spot. And let's say I was years old, I was making $500,000 or $1 million. What happens to most people is that when your actual real results drop below what you believe you're capable of, what do you feel? You feel dissonance. You feel pain. You feel a lack. I say, wait, I'm worthy of this. I should be here, but I'm here. And that then gives you the internal motivation to close the gap to what you believe your reality should be. You understand that? And that's what's really going on with it. So the standard says, I know my, my, logically watch, logically say, fuck, I should be one of the richest people around. I know my, it's how I feel. It's what I believe. It's my belief systems. So now we start talking about beliefs, a separate distinction of the intergame, but the beliefs start indicating my state. It's like a function of what I believe is true, what I logically think should be versus what actual results I'm getting over time. Because what happens is there's only so much pain. Any person's brain can take before you start saying, you know what, maybe I want to lower my standard. You see, if, if you're not getting your result that you think you're capable of, you feel dissonance, you feel pain and that pain motivates you to work harder to change your approach, to keep going, to make sure you hit that number. So that's why I say your standard axiom is almost your insurance policy to make sure that you get what you want in life because you feel bad. You feel uncomfortable because you're below your standard. But what happens if that continues for too long? What happens if your results don't match your standard year in and year out? That's when we see people who talk about, I remember once when I thought I could take over the world and I thought my life would be this way. I thought I'd be richer than I was 21 years old. When I was in high school, I had all these hopes and dreams and now I'm 32, 33. And life, the reality of life has hit me and I feel good. Now I feel pretty good. You know, making 200,000 a year, 100 grand a year, so when I love maybe my kids are healthy and I'm okay and life is okay. But are you really burning bright? You see, then you have that thing for the next thing, but I don't think he might be, you see, I'm not sure that's what's happened to Fernando, but the point is, is that like then, so we convince ourselves to stop the pain, to stop the discomfort of, of not hitting your standard, eventually your brain will lower your standard so you feel good again. See, either you're going to hit your standard or you're going to, and you'll feel good, or eventually if you can't hit your standard, you'll drop your standard because you're sick and tired of the pain of not meeting your standard. So you reduce your standards and say, you know what, I'm still okay.
